title: The Total Cost of AI Ownership (AI TCO) | Cohere
gist: A Cohere thought-leadership post argues that enterprise AI spending (Gartner: $2.52T globally in 2026, +44% YoY) is systematically outrunning cost forecasts across industry surveys, and makes the case that steady-state 'workhorse' inference should be owned rather than rented, citing Lenovo/NVIDIA data showing owned inference at roughly $0.11-0.12 per million tokens versus ~$0.89 (cloud) and ~$2.00 (frontier API) — an 8-18x gap that only holds under sustained high utilization.

insights.claim: Gartner projects global AI spending will reach $2.52 trillion in 2026, a 44% year-over-year increase, driven primarily by infrastructure investment.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Establishes the absolute scale of the AI infrastructure buildout that the TCO/own-vs-rent argument is being made against — a market growing nearly 50% annually.
insights.quote: Gartner projects global AI spending will reach $2.52 trillion USD in 2026, a 44% annual increase, driven primarily by infrastructure.
insights.locator: AI TCO is rising alongside usage

insights.claim: Gartner anticipates AI will enter a 'trough of disillusionment' in which the main barrier to further scaling becomes the unpredictability of financial returns rather than lack of ambition or use cases.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Signals a near-term sentiment risk for AI infrastructure spend and vendor valuations even as absolute dollar spend keeps rising — a gap between hype cycle and ROI realization worth tracking.
insights.quote: Gartner anticipates that AI will enter the "trough of disillusionment," where the main barrier to scaling is the unpredictability of returns, not lack of ambition.
insights.locator: The surprise is already visible across markets

insights.claim: A December 2025 IDC InfoBrief commissioned by DataRobot found that 96% of organizations deploying generative AI and 92% using agentic AI experienced higher-than-expected costs.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Cost overruns are now the industry norm rather than the exception for both generative and agentic AI deployments, indicating systemic under-forecasting rather than isolated mismanagement.
insights.quote: 96% of organizations deploying generative AI and 92% using agentic AI faced higher-than-expected costs.
insights.locator: The surprise is already visible across markets

insights.claim: A November 2025 McKinsey survey of nearly 2,000 organizations across 105 countries found that while AI adoption is widespread, only about one-third of organizations are scaling it enterprise-wide, and just 5-6% report significant financial impact.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Most enterprise AI spend has not yet converted into measurable financial return, making cost discipline (not further spend) the binding constraint on AI's business case at most companies.
insights.quote: only about one-third are scaling it enterprise-wide, and just 5-6% report significant financial impact.
insights.locator: The surprise is already visible across markets

insights.claim: A 2025 Mavvrik and Benchmarkit survey found that 80% of companies miss their AI infrastructure cost forecasts by more than 25%, nearly 25% miss by more than 50%, and only 15% land within 10% of actual spend.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: AI infrastructure budgeting and forecasting discipline is broken at most enterprises today, which is a direct catalyst for demand toward more predictable, owned-infrastructure pricing models like Cohere is pitching.
insights.quote: 80% of companies miss their AI infrastructure forecasts by over 25%, and nearly 25% miss by more than 50%. Only 15% are within 10% of their actual spend.
insights.locator: Forecasting and governance remain challenging

insights.claim: The same Mavvrik/Benchmarkit survey found 84% of firms report gross-margin erosion of 6% or more due to AI infrastructure costs, and for software companies these costs are recurring rather than discretionary innovation spend.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: AI infrastructure cost is becoming a structural margin headwind for software companies broadly, a factor investors should model explicitly into gross-margin forecasts for AI-adopting SaaS businesses.
insights.quote: 84% of firms report gross-margin erosion of 6% or more due to AI infrastructure.
insights.locator: Forecasting and governance remain challenging

insights.claim: Only about a third of firms report their on-premises AI expenses, indicating a visibility gap in cost accounting that compounds the broader forecasting problem across the industry.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Limited on-prem cost reporting likely means industry-wide AI TCO surveys understate true spend, so headline cost-overrun statistics may already be conservative.
insights.locator: Forecasting and governance remain challenging

themes.theme: Uber as a live case study

insights.claim: Uber disclosed on an earnings call that 10% of its committed code is now built by autonomous coding agents, and separately reported spending twelve months' worth of its AI budget in only four months.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: A concrete named example of agentic AI simultaneously delivering visible productivity gains and blowing through budget forecasts 3x faster than planned — a template for how AI ROI and AI cost overrun narratives can coexist at the same company.
insights.quote: an earnings call from Uber stated that “10% of the company’s committed code is built by autonomous agents,” which coincided with the company spending 12 months of its AI budget in only four.
insights.locator: Looking at the AI system at large

themes.theme: Own-vs-rent as the central strategic decision

insights.claim: Cohere argues that firms which scale AI profitably will not be those that spend the most, but those that determine which AI capabilities to own versus rent and build organizational discipline to tell the two apart.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Reframes AI cost management from a spend-optimization problem into a capital-allocation and make-vs-buy decision, which is the core commercial argument for Cohere's owned-deployment product lines.
insights.quote: Firms that scale AI profitably will not be those that spend the most, but those that work out which AI capabilities to own and which to rent — and build the discipline to tell them apart.
insights.locator: Layers of AI ownership: Renting and owning infrastructure

insights.claim: A model, chips, or data center that remains entirely under a third-party vendor's control constitutes a rented capability regardless of how much a company spends on it, exposing the buyer to repricing, rate-limiting, deprecation, or government-driven restriction risk.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Reframes vendor dependency in AI as a strategic and geopolitical exposure, not merely a cost line item — relevant for enterprises and governments assessing AI supply-chain risk amid tightening export controls.
insights.quote: A capability that can be repriced, rate-limited, deprecated, or restricted by another company — or another country — is rented, however much is spent on it.
insights.locator: AI ownership in practice

insights.claim: Utilization — not raw token price — is identified as the single most important variable determining whether owning or renting AI infrastructure is more cost-effective for a given workload.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Gives enterprises a concrete decision variable (utilization rate) to model when choosing between committing to owned hardware and paying per-token API rates.
insights.quote: Utilization is the primary factor in determining whether owning or renting is more cost-effective.
insights.locator: Capital and operating costs of AI

insights.claim: NVIDIA argues that cost-per-token — not compute cost or theoretical operations-per-dollar — is the metric that matters for AI infrastructure ROI, since a cheaper GPU that produces fewer tokens per second can end up costing more per token than a pricier one.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Cost-per-token is presented as the correct unit economics metric for evaluating GPU purchasing decisions, over simpler but misleading metrics like sticker price or FLOPs/dollar.
insights.quote: a cheaper GPU that delivers fewer tokens per second ends up costing more per token, not less.
insights.locator: Reduce AI costs through efficient deployment

insights.claim: NVIDIA reports its Blackwell-class systems deliver roughly 50 times more tokens per megawatt than the prior Hopper generation, cutting cost per token by about 35 times.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: A large generational efficiency jump materially changes owned-infrastructure economics between GPU generations, meaning TCO models must be refreshed with each hardware cycle rather than treated as static.
insights.quote: NVIDIA reports that its Blackwell-class systems deliver roughly 50 times more tokens per megawatt than the previous Hopper generation, cutting cost per token by about 35 times.
insights.locator: Reduce AI costs through efficient deployment

insights.claim: Mixture-of-experts routing, used in Cohere's models, lowers generation cost by activating only a subset of parameters per token without significant capability loss, while low-bit quantization further increases throughput and reduces latency.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Positions model-level architectural efficiency (MoE, quantization, right-sized model routing) as a controllable lever in AI TCO that enterprises can act on independent of hardware choice.
insights.locator: Reduce AI costs through efficient deployment

insights.claim: A 2026 Lenovo analysis of 8-GPU servers running 70-405 billion parameter models put the amortized cost of generating a million tokens at roughly $0.11 on owned H100 hardware, versus about $0.89 for an equivalent cloud instance and around $2.00 for a comparable frontier API.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Quantifies the headline own-vs-rent gap (roughly 8x versus cloud, up to 18x versus a frontier API) that underpins the entire ownership argument, assuming sustained high utilization.
insights.quote: roughly $0.11 on owned H100 hardware, against about $0.89 for the equivalent cloud instance and around $2.00 for a comparable frontier API.
insights.locator: the gap shows up in hard numbers

insights.claim: NVIDIA separately reports $0.123 per million tokens on its GB300 platform, measured by the third-party SemiAnalysis InferenceX benchmark, landing in roughly the same $0.11-$0.12 range as Lenovo's independent five-year H100 ownership estimate.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Two independently derived estimates converging on the same owned-inference cost range gives the ownership cost argument more credibility than either figure alone, though the article notes the methods aren't strictly comparable.
insights.locator: Two independent estimates, reached by different methods

insights.claim: The Lenovo analysis found an 8-GPU owned server pays for itself in under four months when compared against on-demand cloud pricing (the most expensive rental option), but ownership only remains cheaper than renting if the hardware is used more than roughly four hours per day.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete utilization threshold (roughly 4 hours/day) below which the ownership case collapses — a decision rule enterprises can apply directly when choosing between owned hardware and cloud rental.
insights.quote: owning an 8-GPU box turns out to be cheaper than renting one with only roughly four hours of use per day.
insights.locator: Two things temper these figures

insights.claim: Cohere itself cautions that NVIDIA's cost-per-token framing is self-serving (it showcases NVIDIA's own chips) and that the cited cloud benchmarks omit real-world costs like storage, egress, and support, meaning the true owned-versus-rented cost gap may be narrower than the headline 8-18x figures suggest.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: An explicit hedge inside an otherwise pro-ownership piece — investors and buyers should treat the 8-18x savings figures as an upper bound rather than a guaranteed outcome.
insights.quote: analysts noting the metric flatters highly optimized, homogeneous environments more than messy, real-world ones.
insights.locator: Two things temper these figures

insights.claim: AI infrastructure costs split into capital expenditure (chips, servers, storage, networking — owned assets depreciated over time) and operating expenditure (cloud instances, tokens, power, cooling — metered resources that are easy to start but become significant at scale), with throughput, unit price, responsiveness, and utilization as the key cost drivers spanning both.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: Gives a structured checklist (four cost drivers, two spending categories) that finance leaders can apply to build an AI TCO model rather than relying on vendor-quoted token prices alone.
insights.locator: Capital and operating costs of AI

insights.claim: Cohere argues the cloud remains best suited for bursts of training, experimentation, and unpredictable demand, while steady-state 'workhorse' inference increasingly favors owned infrastructure — implying a hybrid own/rent split rather than an all-cloud or all-on-prem strategy.
insights.speaker: Cohere Blog
insights.so_what: The practical takeaway for enterprise buyers: keep bursty/experimental workloads on cloud APIs, but move steady, high-utilization production inference to owned or dedicated infrastructure to capture the bulk of the TCO savings.
insights.quote: The cloud still wins at what it was built for: bursts of training, experimentation, and unpredictable demand. But the workhorse inference that drives long-run cost increasingly belongs closer to home.
insights.locator: closing paragraph
